title: GMAT vs GRE: Which Exam Should You Take for Business School?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have decided to apply to business school. Now you face a choice that did not exist a decade ago: should you take the GMAT or the GRE? Most business schools now accept both, and the decision can significantly affect your preparation timeline, your testing experience, and your competitiveness as an applicant. There is no universally correct answer. The right exam for you depends on your strengths, your career goals, and the specific schools you are targeting. Here is how to decide.

How the Two Exams Differ

The GMAT Focus Edition, introduced in 2024, has three sections: Quantitative Reasoning, Verbal Reasoning, and Data Insights. It does not have an essay or a geometry component. The exam is computer-adaptive and is designed specifically for business school admissions. The total score ranges from 205 to 805.

The GRE General Test has three sections: Verbal Reasoning, Quantitative Reasoning, and Analytical Writing. The Verbal and Quantitative sections are scored on a scale of 130 to 170. The Analytical Writing section is scored on a scale of 0 to 6. The GRE is not business-school specific. It is used for admissions to a wide range of graduate programs, from engineering to humanities to business.

The core difference is emphasis. The GMAT is designed to test the skills that business schools value: logical reasoning, data analysis, and quantitative problem-solving. Its question types, particularly Data Sufficiency and Data Insights, reflect the kinds of analytical tasks you will encounter in a business curriculum. The GRE is a broader test of verbal and quantitative reasoning with a writing component. Its verbal section is generally considered more vocabulary-intensive than the GMAT. Its quantitative section is generally considered less challenging.

When the GMAT Is the Better Choice

Take the GMAT if you are applying exclusively to business schools. The GMAT is still the preferred exam for many MBA programs, particularly at the most competitive schools. While admissions committees say they have no preference, some employers, particularly in consulting and investment banking, ask for GMAT scores during the recruiting process. A strong GMAT score can serve you beyond the admissions decision.

Take the GMAT if you are strong in logical reasoning and data analysis. The GMAT's unique question types reward test-takers who can evaluate arguments, assess the sufficiency of information, and extract insights from data presented in multiple formats. If these skills are your strengths, the GMAT will showcase them.

Take the GMAT if you prefer a shorter exam. The GMAT Focus Edition takes approximately two hours and fifteen minutes. The GRE takes approximately three hours and forty-five minutes. The time difference is significant, especially if you struggle with test-taking stamina.

When the GRE Is the Better Choice

Take the GRE if you are applying to both business schools and other graduate programs. The GRE is accepted by a wide range of programs, giving you flexibility if you are considering dual degrees or are not certain that business school is your only path. A single GRE score can serve multiple applications.

Take the GRE if you are strong in vocabulary and writing. The GRE verbal section is more vocabulary-dependent than the GMAT, and the Analytical Writing section allows you to demonstrate your ability to construct a written argument. If these skills are your strengths, the GRE will showcase them better than the GMAT.

Take the GRE if you find the GMAT's question types uncomfortable. The GRE's question formats are more traditional. You solve problems and answer questions. There is no Data Sufficiency and no multi-source reasoning. If the GMAT's unique question types create anxiety rather than opportunity, the GRE may be a better fit.

Take the GRE if you need more flexibility in your test-taking strategy. The GRE allows you to skip questions and return to them within a section. The GMAT is computer-adaptive, meaning you must answer each question before moving to the next and cannot return to previous questions. The GRE's format gives you more control over your pacing.

What Business Schools Actually Think

Admissions committees genuinely accept both exams. This is not a situation where schools say they accept both but secretly prefer the GMAT. The vast majority of MBA programs have no preference. What matters is your score relative to other applicants who took the same exam. A high GRE score is as valuable as a high GMAT score. A low score on either will weaken your application.

If you are unsure which exam to take, take a diagnostic test for both. The GMAC offers a free GMAT practice test. ETS offers a free GRE practice test. Take both under timed conditions. Compare your scores using the concordance tables published by the testing organizations. Your performance on the diagnostic tests will tell you which exam plays to your strengths.

The Bottom Line

The choice between the GMAT and the GRE depends on your target schools, your career goals, and your personal strengths. Take the GMAT if you are applying exclusively to business schools, are strong in logic and data analysis, and prefer a shorter exam. Take the GRE if you are applying to multiple types of programs, are strong in vocabulary and writing, or prefer more flexible pacing. Take diagnostic tests for both before deciding. The right exam is the one on which you will score higher relative to other test-takers. Your score matters more than which exam you took to earn it.
